attributes.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic concerned about yellowish patches around her eyes that have been present for two years and are gradually enlarging. She is embarrassed by their appearance and asks about removal options. Her medical history includes hypertension treated with perindopril 5 mg daily and a family history of premature coronary artery disease (her father had a myocardial infarction at age 52). She does not take any other regular medications. She is a non-smoker with minimal alcohol intake. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are symmetrical, soft, yellowish plaques on the medial aspects of her upper and lower eyelids bilaterally (Image). Her body mass index is 27 kg/m². Investigations reveal: total cholesterol 5.8 mmol/L (3.9-5.5), low-density lipoprotein 3.9 mmol/L (2.0-3.4), high-density lipoprotein 1.2 mmol/L (1.0-2.2), triglycerides 1.5 mmol/L (0.5-1.7), and fasting glucose 5.2 mmol/L (3.9-5.5).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management approach for this patient?
options: Reassurance that the lesions are benign with no further action required
Referral for surgical excision without additional intervention
Dietary modification and statin therapy with consideration of later cosmetic intervention
Topical trichloroacetic acid as first-line treatment
Referral for laser therapy as definitive management
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with xanthelasma, which are cholesterol-rich deposits in the periorbital skin. While they are benign lesions, they have important clinical implications beyond their cosmetic impact. The patient has mildly elevated lipid levels, a family history of premature coronary artery disease, and hypertension, all of which contribute to her cardiovascular risk profile.

Dietary modification and statin therapy with consideration of later cosmetic intervention is the most appropriate approach. This addresses both the underlying metabolic disorder and the cosmetic concern. Statins are indicated in this patient due to her elevated LDL cholesterol and increased cardiovascular risk (family history of premature coronary artery disease and hypertension). Studies have shown that treating the underlying dyslipidemia can lead to regression of xanthelasma in some patients and reduce the risk of recurrence after removal. Once lipid levels are optimized, cosmetic interventions can be considered with a lower risk of recurrence.

Reassurance that the lesions are benign with no further action is inadequate management. While xanthelasma are indeed benign, they are markers of cardiovascular risk even in patients with normal lipid profiles. This patient has elevated LDL cholesterol and additional cardiovascular risk factors that warrant intervention. Furthermore, the patient is concerned about the cosmetic appearance, which impacts her quality of life.

Referral for surgical excision without additional intervention addresses only the cosmetic aspect without treating the underlying metabolic disorder. Surgical excision of xanthelasma has a high recurrence rate (up to 40%) if underlying dyslipidemia is not adequately controlled. Additionally, surgery carries risks of scarring, ectropion, and other complications in the delicate periorbital area.

Topical trichloroacetic acid is one of several local treatments for xanthelasma, but it should not be used as first-line therapy without addressing the underlying dyslipidemia. Chemical treatments can cause hypopigmentation, scarring, and have variable efficacy. They are generally considered after medical management of lipid abnormalities has been optimized.

Referral for laser therapy as definitive management, like surgical excision, addresses only the cosmetic aspect. Laser therapy (CO2, Er:YAG, or pulsed dye lasers) can be effective for xanthelasma but also has recurrence rates of 25-50% if underlying dyslipidemia is not controlled. Additionally, multiple sessions may be required, and there are risks of scarring and pigmentary changes.

The optimal management of xanthelasma involves a comprehensive approach that addresses both the underlying metabolic disorder and the cosmetic concern. This includes lifestyle modifications (diet, exercise, weight management), lipid-lowering therapy when indicated, and consideration of cosmetic procedures after metabolic optimization. The patient should be counseled that xanthelasma are markers of cardiovascular risk and that treating the underlying dyslipidemia is important for both cardiovascular health and reducing the risk of recurrence after cosmetic intervention.
learningPoints: Xanthelasma are associated with increased cardiovascular risk even in patients with normal lipid profiles.
Management of xanthelasma should address both cosmetic concerns and underlying cardiovascular risk factors.
Surgical excision of xanthelasma has a high recurrence rate if underlying dyslipidemia is not adequately controlled.
references: Nair PA, Singhal R. Xanthelasma palpebrarum - a brief review. Clinical Cosmetic Investigation Dermatology. 2017 Dec 18;11:1-5. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC5739544/

Sun M, Huang S, Huilgol S, Selva D. Eyelid lesions in general practice. Australian Journal of General Practice. 2019:48(8). Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2019/august/eyelid-lesions-in-general-practice

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
